misconceptionSummary: Students treat subtraction as commutative, writing a − b = b − a.

steps.narration: Addition is commutative — swapping the order of the numbers never changes the total. Three plus five and five plus three both equal eight.

lines.text: a − b ≠ b − a  in general

steps.narration: Subtraction is not commutative. Eight minus three gives five, but three minus eight gives negative five. Reversing the order changes the result — and the sign.

lines.text: Addition and subtraction undo each other

steps.narration: Addition and subtraction are inverse operations — each undoes the other. If you add four to seven to get eleven, subtracting four from eleven gets you back to seven. Use this to check any addition or subtraction.

visuals.expression: a + b = b + a,  but  a − b ≠ b − a
visuals.label: Addition: commutative. Subtraction: order matters.

steps.narration: Addition is commutative; subtraction is not. And the two operations are inverses — addition undoes subtraction and vice versa.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: assuming 8 − 3 = 3 − 8. Subtraction is not commutative — the order matters. Only addition allows you to swap the numbers freely.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: You can swap the order in addition but never in subtraction. Eight minus three is five; three minus eight is negative five — completely different answers.
